sentences.text: My name is Bert Strauss.
I was born in Germany in 19-- April the 29th, 1925.
And my given name at that time was Berthold Ludwig Strauss.
I was born in Gemunden an der Wohra, which is in the district of Kassel.
My Jewish name was Baruch ben Yitchzak, named after my grandfather-- father rather, who had lived in Gemunden since 1863, I believe it was.
My mother is from Gemunden.
My father was born in the southern part of Germany in a small little town called Billigheim in [INAUDIBLE], Mosbach.
And he married into this house.
The house I was born in and lived in had been occupied by my parents, grandparents, great-grandparents, and their parents before that.
I believe to the best of my knowledge, the family lived in that house since the early 1800s, or possibly even the late 1780s.
In any event, I had a very nice childhood.
We lived in this small little town.
My parents were in the general goods business.
We had a little store, as well as a farm.
And we work the farm, as well as did business out of the store.
And I helped and I was exposed to both of these enterprises as a child.
And obviously, it gave me a lot of insight into what business was all about, as well as what agriculture was about.

My childhood from the age of three-- I can remember that far back-- till the age of about six or seven was, I would say, a kind of childhood any child might have wanted to have-- serene, loving, caring parents and grandparents.
I had two aunts living in the house where I lived.
And in 1926, I had a-- a little sister was born into our family.
Her name was Greta.
And as far as I can remember, we always had a wonderful, warm relationship.
Our family was a fairly orthodox family, orthodox Jewish family.
We used to go to minyan every day.
Although the town itself had only 16 Jewish families, nevertheless it was a cohesive [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] group of people living in that town.
And we really were family with everybody else.
Things changed, of course, as time went on in 1932 and 1933.
Hitler was elected, or put into power.
And for what reason I really don't know and I haven't really explored it and looked into it, Gemunden and the whole general area where I lived became extremely Nazified, if, if that's a word.
There was lots of unemployment.

But in any event, people in Gemunden, in my town, those who had been friends with us and our family, suddenly because they were promised, I guess, a better life from Nazi officials became our enemies.

I remember back in 1933 and 1934 when the first boycotts took place in Gemunden, when the brown shirts, or SA, were standing in front of our house, posters put on our windows and doors-- don't buy by Jews, don't patronize Jews.
It was a very-- it was a terrible experience for me, because that was the first time I felt being persecuted.
I always knew I was a Jew, obviously, because the rest of the community was Christian.
But I never really knew I was any different from the Christians other than I was Jewish.
And all of sudden I was an outcast.
In 1932, I started going to school-- 1931, excuse me. 1931, I started going to school.
And that was German public school.
Things were fairly decent yet in those days.
But in '33, things really started to get bad.
There wasn't a day I wasn't picked on.
There wasn't a day that one or another child would call me dirty Jew.
There wasn't a day that one or another child would pick on me, hit me.
And things became progressively worse.
In 1934, 1935, things were so bad that I couldn't even go home by myself anymore from school.
My mother had to pick me up.
And even with my mother presence, at times, children would, would beat me up and call me names.
Things became very difficult.

In 1934, because of the problems we had in the German schools and also of a program, I suppose, which the Nazis initiated, we Jewish children-- there were five or six of us in the school-- were expelled from the school.
And a, quote, 'Jewish school' was opened with a teacher, who actually was not a teacher, but it was our chazan.
It was the, the man who would sing and perform the, the prayers in synagogue, or lead the prayers.
We did not have a lot rabbi.
In 1936-- and, and that school was open in '34, I believe.
And it went on till 1936.
In 1936, all funds from the German government were taken away from the Jewish school.
And the school was closed.
From 1936 to 1937, I was at home and not in any school at all.
Yes?
My parents, of course, were very protective of us.
And my father, having been a extreme optimist all his life, always felt that this situation and condition wouldn't go on for very long.
Situation was a temporary situation and that Hitler would very soon be kicked out of the government.
And things would change for us.
There was a-- a tremendous amount of harassment of my parents.
And there were very, very few weeks where the Nazis wouldn't go by our house at night and throw rocks and stones into our windows.
I can remember incidents where rocks, like probably weighing two or three pounds, were thrown through our windows and wound up in my bed.
Very, very scary.
And I would say that even to this day-- I'm 70 years old-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] if I hear a strange noise at night, I, I have a flashback.
It takes me back to my childhood days, 1933, 1934, and '35 and '36 and '37 when these incidents occurred.
And obviously I know I am here at 1995, and those things couldn't happen here.
In any event, my father was kicked out of business.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He wasn't kicked out of business.
He was boycotted to the point that he had to give up his business.
And he eventually started working for other people.
Things became very difficult.
The farm, which we had had been worked by us.
And we always had hired help to do the work my father and mother could not do, weren't able to do.
These people who had been working for us were told they could not work any longer for us.
We had to get rid of our animals.
And eventually, we had to get rid of all the farm land we had by literally giving it away.
We had to sell it.
But it had to be sold to certain people who were Nazi-- people who were active in the-- in the Nazi party.
And they were giving preference as far as what land they could buy from Jews.
And, of course, the price was dictated by, by the government, what we could sell it for. 1937-- from 1936 to 1937, I didn't go to school.
And in 1937, the middle of the year, a school was opened up in a town near Frankfurt, Bad Nauheim.
This school was to serve children like myself and approximately 250 other children in the general area where I lived in the Kassel district who couldn't go to school anymore.

And this became a Jewish school, where some of us who live closed enough to-- but now I would commute every day-- and others who lived further away, like I did, were staying at this school overnight during the school year.
And then, of course, during the vacation time we would go home.
Yes.
My sister at the time experienced the same hardships I did.
And obviously, when Bad Nauheim school opened up, she went also to Bad Nauheim.
In Bad Nauheim, school education was excellent.
Most of our teachers were young teachers who had just graduated from the teacher seminar in Wurzburg.
And there was some older teachers who had been teachers in the German elementary and higher schools of learning.
And they couldn't teach there anymore.
Consequently, they needed a place to work.
And they were teaching in our schools.
So the staff we had was excellent.
And the experience I had in Bad Nauheim was an excellent experience, which, to this day, is still with me.
I still fondly think of the days in Bad Nauheim.
Number one, we weren't harassed.
We were amongst Jewish children.

And our Jewish education, as such, as far as orthodoxy and so on is concerned, was perpetuated and, and very, very strongly [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] given, given an issue, where where all of us were exposed, even those children who had not been used for-- to, to orthodoxy from home were exposed to Judaism, not only Judaism but also Zionism, which was the first time I was exposed to it and came into the picture.
Well, the groups which were formed were several Zionist groups, was Habonim and Agudat Yisrael.
And there were people who had come from Palestine, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who were teaching courses in our school about settling of Palestine and, of course, the principles of, of Zionism.

And they felt and, of course, the community felt the only place to be and go to have a better future for all of us was to go to Palestine and build, build a country for ourselves, where things which happened in Germany could not happen.
Things went, went along fine in Bad Nauheim obviously till 1938, November the 9th, 1938, during the Kristallnacht.
Unawares of what was going on politically-- after all, we were still children.
We're 12 and 13 years old.
There were younger children there and older children there.
Around 10 o'clock on November the 9th, brown shirts and SS came through the school.
And I, I still can see it in front of my eyes.
They walked into the classroom where I was.
And that particular classroom held a Torah, the Sefer Torah, where we used to conduct our services.
We had minyan every morning, a minyan being the village's services wearing tefillin.
They took the Torah out of the ark and just tore it apart and officially declared school closed.
And everybody, including our teachers, were taken into the outside-- in, into the area where we usually did our physical activities.
And we were marched out of that area into the police station in Bad Nauheim.
And we were kept there till about 3 o'clock in the afternoon.
And nobody knew really what was going on.
It was a very, very-- we were very distraught.
There was very little support.
Our men teachers were taken away from us.
And they were arrested.
We didn't know where they were or where they went to.
And the lady teachers and women teachers we had didn't know what was happening to the men either.
So it was a very difficult day, that particular day.
But about 3 or 4 o'clock in the afternoon, we-- excuse me-- [DRINKS] we were marched back to the home where we had stayed in and were told to pack our suitcases, that the school was closed.
And we were sent back home, which had to be the next day, which was November the 10th.
No, no, my parents were not informed.
We were put on a train.
And obviously, I did not know what was happening, what had happened.
And I came home to Gemunden, walked home from the railroad station to my house.
And our house was in shambles.
It was absolutely torn apart from top to bottom.
My mother and my aunts were there, received me, were very distraught.
And I said, where's my father?
Where's my dad?
Well, papa isn't here.
Well, where is papa.
Papa is not feeling well.
Well, what's the matter with him?
And they slowly told me that the night before, November the 9th, that they broke into our house, Nazis broke into our house, tore everything apart.
And they beat my father so badly that he had to go to the hospital.
And I asked, of course, where was he?
What hospital was he in?
Because there was no hospital in Gemunden, they had taken him to Marburg, which is approximately 30 kilometers from where we lived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I immediately got on my bicycle.
And there was no transportation other than a train going to Marburg, but only once a day. 6-- it would leave 6 o'clock in the morning.
And I rode to Marburg.
I was 13 years old, about 31 kilometers away from Gemunden, and visited my father in the hospital.
And I can never forget the sight I saw when I saw my father.
He looked like a mummy.
His face was bandaged.
His head was bandaged.
He had skull fractures, lacerations in his face.
He had broken ribs, broken arm, broken leg.
And that all happened in that night, during that night, November the 9th, when they broke into our house.
What's the reason for it?
We were Jews.
And the people who did it were Nazis, who had no regard for us anymore, although we had been their neighbors, their friends, gone together to school with them.
My mother went to school in Gemunden.
My grandfather went to school in Gemunden.
And there was a town of approximately 1,100 people.
We knew everyone.
And everybody knew us.
And yet, those people who had been our acquaintances and friends turned around and tried to kill us, ready to do away with us.
Well, I stayed with my father that day.
I slept on the floor next to his bed.
And the next morning, I went back by bicycle to Gemunden again.
My mother and my aunts who lived with us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I, I don't know.
I can't find a word for it, for the condition, mental condition they were in.
And they had been beaten too, not as severely, obviously, as my father.
But they did beat them.
And I said, well, what are we going to do now?
Well, there's nothing we can do, my mother said, because we have no place to go.
We have to stay here.
At least we have to stay here till your father comes back.
Things became very, very difficult.
Food started running low.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had no income.
We had no money, because whatever money we had in our savings or bank accounts had been frozen.
We had no access to it.
And things were very, very, very difficult.
Nevertheless, there were some neighbors, some Christians, some people, who left at night bread and butter and eggs and milk and potatoes on our doorsteps on our house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did not want to be recognized.
They didn't want to be known, because they knew that under conditions and circumstances these things were happening, if they exposed themselves to who they were, they would be punished for it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm forever grateful for those people, to those people who helped us.
Some of them were alive when I came back from the concentration camp.
But I'll get into that a little later.
Obviously, in 1938, in November the 9th, our synagogue was also torn down and burned.
And during the process of the tearing down and desecrating our synagogue, we had three Torah in our ark in Gemunden, which must have been there for, I don't know, a hundred years or more.
The Torahs were taken out of the synagogue and desecrated, thrown out onto the street and torn apart and walked on.

When I came back-- just to put this in-- when I came back in 1945 to Gemunden after four years in concentration camp, some of these people who had helped us during these difficult days in 1938 and '39 invited me to their house.
They were two women who had gone to school with my mother.
And they said to me, Ludwig, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we have something for you.
We have hidden it since 1938.
And I'm sure you would appreciate having it.
And one of the women went upstairs, and brought this down for me.
This is part of a Sefer Torah, which they picked up during the night of November the 8th.
They were very religious Christians, knew the significance of the Torah, what it meant to us.
At the risk of being caught, took it home with them and hid it till I came back.
On the back side of this piece of scroll, you will see dirt and the footsteps of the Nazis, who desecrated our, our synagogue and did this to the Torah.
I'll always keep this and remind me and my children, hopefully their children, of what had happened in Germany, that nothing was sacred.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In-- But as time went on, in 1938, when my father came back from the hospital in I believe it was the early part in December.
And he recuperated.
And by January of 1939, my father and I were directed to go to work to build a underground-- be a laborer-- to build an underground airport for the German army or air force at that time.
This airport was approximately 35 kilometers away from my hometown.
We had to get up every morning at 4 o'clock, go to work.
And the type of work, kind of labor we performed was carrying bricks.
From 7 o'clock in the morning till 5:00 in the afternoon, my dad and I, as well as other Jews who were there, carried bricks.
There we no dollies to carry them on.
We had no protective clothing to wear, no gloves to wear to protect our hands.
And obviously, having not been used to it-- and I was a little boy.
I was 13 years old-- my hands were very tender.
And after the first day, I had bloody hands carrying these bricks approximately maybe 400, 500 yards from one place to another.

Nevertheless, it was not an excuse not to go to work the next day, because our food rations we were getting and my parents-- my mother was getting, my aunts were getting-- dependent on my father and myself going to work and doing that type of work.
It was very difficult, very hard.
But as I said earlier, my father always had a positive attitude, which fortunately I inherited from him.
And we talked a lot to each other and said, we just have to keep on going.
Hopefully things change.
I don't really know if any one, my father, made any serious effort to leave or to-- to escape.
I know there were some efforts made to immigrate to the United States.
But as I was told then, as I understood then-- that we had a quota number assigned to us from the American consulate in Stuttgart to immigrate to the United States.
There were x number of people allowed to immigrate to the United States.
And in order for us to get to the United States under that number, which was issued to us, we would've had to wait till 1945.
